Title: Cytochromes P450 for engineering herbicide tolerance

Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast stroma (after addition of chloroplast transit peptide in transgenic plants)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates the proherbicide R7402 in plants, causing phytotoxicity; used as a negative selection marker and for tapetum-specific male sterility
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in tobacco; chloroplast targeting; tissue-specific promoter expression; herbicide/proherbicide treatment phenotyping
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SU1/CYP105A1 from Streptomyces griseolus metabolizes sulfonylurea compounds. 2. Chloroplast-targeted expression in tobacco activates the otherwise harmless proherbicide R7402 into a toxic herbicide. 3. Whole-plant expression causes plant death after R7402 treatment, whereas tapetum-specific expression causes male sterility, enabling negative selection applications.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Confers selectable toxicity for negative selection and conditional male sterility systems
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a negative selection marker, for conditional male sterility, and biotechnology selection systems
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review paper; SU1 is summarized as an applied herbicide-metabolizing P450 rather than newly discovered in this article.

Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Thylakoid membranes/chloroplast-targeted in fusion constructs; microsomal P450 by origin
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Hydroxylated/dealkylated herbicide metabolites of atrazine, chlortoluron, pyriminobac-methyl
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Confers herbicide tolerance in transgenic plants by broad-spectrum herbicide metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in tobacco and potato; fusion to yeast P450 reductase; chloroplast targeting; herbicide tolerance assays
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Rat CYP1A1 metabolizes several herbicides with broad substrate specificity, including atrazine, chlortoluron and pyriminobac-methyl. 2. It catalyzes both ring-methyl hydroxylation and N-dealkylation reactions. 3. Expression in tobacco and potato generated herbicide-tolerant plants, and activity was improved by fusion with yeast P450 reductase and chloroplast targeting.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Detoxification of herbicides for crop protection
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Demonstrates feasibility of engineering herbicide tolerance, though mammalian origin raises ethical concerns
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ethical concern because CYP1A1 can activate polyaromatic hydrocarbons into procarcinogens
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core summarized engineering gene in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes dealkylation of several planar xenobiotics including phenylurea; converts phenylurea herbicides to di-N-dealkylated non-phytotoxic products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Herbicide detoxification and increased plant tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Inducible by Mn2+ ions, aminopyrine, and phenobarbital
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation based on inducibility; heterologous expression in yeast with Arabidopsis P450 reductase; enzyme activity assay; tobacco overexpression; herbicide tolerance phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP76B1 from Helianthus tuberosus efficiently dealkylates phenylurea herbicides in yeast assays. 2. It produces non-phytotoxic di-N-dealkylated products with high turnover comparable to physiological substrates. 3. Overexpression in tobacco increases tolerance to chlortoluron and linuron.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes herbicide detoxification and increases tolerance to phenylurea herbicides
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising plant-derived catalyst for engineering herbicide tolerance with likely better acceptability than mammalian genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also confers increased resistance to linuron; reviewed as one of the more efficient plant herbicide-metabolizing P450s.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Phenylurea detoxification via ring-methyl hydroxylation and mono-N-demethylation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chlortoluron to ring-methyl hydroxylated products and, to a lesser extent, mono-N-demethylated products; other phenylurea are mainly mono-N-demethylated
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Ring-methyl hydroxylated and mono-N-demethylated phenylurea metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Herbicide detoxification associated with increased tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Isolation using P450-conserved sequences; heterologous expression in yeast; metabolite profiling; tobacco overexpression; herbicide tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71A10 from soybean metabolizes chlortoluron by ring-methyl hydroxylation and weaker mono-N-demethylation. 2. It metabolizes other phenylurea mainly by mono-N-demethylation. 3. Although less efficient than CYP76B1, overexpression in tobacco still increases phenylurea tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases tolerance to phenylurea herbicides through oxidative metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate plant gene for herbicide-tolerance engineering, especially for phenylurea herbicides
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic turnover with phenylurea is reported to be over tenfold lower than CYP76B1.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Phenylpropanoid biosynthesis; side activity in herbicide metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Phenylpropanoid pathway / side activity on phenylurea
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: fortuitous side activity on herbicide detoxification
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ring-methyl hydroxylation of chlortoluron at very low efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression in yeast; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP73A1, primarily a phenylpropanoid enzyme, can ring-methyl hydroxylate chlortoluron in yeast. 2. The activity is detectable but too low to confer meaningful herbicide resistance. 3. It exemplifies physiological P450s with incidental xenobiotic-metabolizing capacity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Minor chlortoluron hydroxylation with insufficient efficiency for significant tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Main physiological role is phenylpropanoid biosynthesis, not herbicide detoxification.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Oxygenated fatty acid metabolism; side activity in herbicide metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Oxygenated fatty acid pathway / side activity on phenylurea
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: fortuitous side activity on herbicide detoxification
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ring-methyl hydroxylation of chlortoluron at low efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression in yeast; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP81B1 from Jerusalem artichoke catalyzes ring-methyl hydroxylation of chlortoluron in recombinant assays. 2. The reaction efficiency is low. 3. The activity is considered insufficient for significant in vivo herbicide detoxification.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Minor herbicide-metabolizing activity without strong tolerance effect
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Main function is inferred to be in oxygenated fatty acid biosynthesis rather than herbicide metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: metabolizes multiple herbicides associated with tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Appears capable of metabolizing chlortoluron, bentazon, chlorimuron, and the insecticide malathion
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic analysis from tolerant maize line; preliminary recombinant evidence
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Genetic analysis suggested that chlortoluron and bentazon metabolism in maize may be controlled by the same gene. 2. A CYP72 from a tolerant maize line appears able to metabolize several herbicides and malathion. 3. Turnover-rate data were not available, so functional strength remains preliminary.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely promotes broad herbicide tolerance in maize
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence is preliminary and unpublished in the review; catalytic efficiency not reported.
